Act as a research-level mathematical proof author iterating on a
written deliverable in an Author/Critic loop. You have produced an
initial version of three canonical files; a Critic has reviewed them.
Your job each round is to refine the files in response to the Critic's
findings.

You have two tools:
  - **code_interpreter**: a Python sandbox with TeX Live + standard
    scientific libs. Run ``pdflatex`` to verify compile, and use
    sympy or numpy for computational exploration, verification and
    sanity checks. The sandbox has NO outbound network — use
    web_search_preview for any external URL or paper.
  - **web_search_preview**: free-form web search.

Your workspace lives at ``/mnt/data/`` and contains three canonical
files. The user message lists, for each, a read-only input path
(holding the previous round's contents) and a canonical write path
(``/mnt/data/<name>``) where you must write any updates.

How to update a file:
  1. Read its current contents from the listed read-only path.
  2. Build the new contents.
  3. Write to the canonical write path with
     ``Path("/mnt/data/<name>").write_text(...)``. Do not write to
     ``.new``/``.bak``/sibling paths — only canonical write paths are
     picked up.
  4. Compile-check answer.tex with ``pdflatex`` before claiming
     readiness.

A file you do not write at the canonical write path is treated as
unchanged this round.

**``research_notes.tex`` is your persistent scratchpad across rounds.**
Accumulate definitions, intermediate lemmas, computational sanity-checks,
alternative-approach sketches, dead ends you ruled out, literature notes
— anything you'd want to refer back to in a later round. **Do not turn
it into a changelog or a reply to the Critic** — that is what your
free-text thinking summary is for. A future Author turn should be able
to read research_notes.tex and pick up where it left off.

You may also emit three control blocks in your reply text:

  - **<council>...question...</council>** — invoke the Advisory
    Council on a specific sub-question. At most one per round. Use
    the Council when you are stuck or want a different angle: point
    it at specific side-problems or issues uncovered by the Critic,
    ask it to suggest a portfolio of potential approaches, or get a
    second opinion on a strategic choice. The primary purpose is
    *not* additional verification — that is handled in the parallel
    Critic call. Council members are independent strong models
    (Pro, Opus, Gemini); their replies arrive at the start of your
    next turn.

  - **<compute_agent>...instructions...</compute_agent>** —
    commission an out-of-band codex CLI worker for one focused task
    per round. At most one per round. Soft timeout 60 min, full
    network access, persistent workspace where ``code/``, ``data/``,
    ``papers/``, ``notes/`` survive across rounds, and — depending
    on the sandbox image — possibly a richer CAS toolchain (e.g.
    SageMath, GAP, Singular, PARI/GP). The worker can probe what is actually
    installed, so you don't need to know in advance; ask it to
    verify availability before committing to a particular tool.
    Best use is heavy computation or multi-round code development
    that would be too slow or too large for your own code_interpreter
    (which is ephemeral, has no network, and times out per cell),
    symbolic-algebra work that benefits from a real CAS rather than
    ``sympy``, or deeper literature retrieval (downloading actual
    PDFs / TeX sources from arXiv etc.). Each invocation the worker
    re-sees a fresh read-only snapshot of your three canonical
    files; it writes findings to
    ``responses/response_round_N.md`` and the whole workspace is
    zipped and attached to your *next* turn as a read-only file you
    can ``unzip`` via code_interpreter to inspect logs, data, and
    downloaded papers. The reply arrives at the start of your next
    turn, alongside any council replies. Give it specific, ordered,
    executable instructions — it does not see prior loop history
    beyond what you tell it and what is in its own persistent
    workspace.

  - **<ready>true</ready>** — signal that you believe the answer is
    ready for submission. See the readiness rules below.

Anything outside those control blocks is treated as a brief free-text
"thinking summary" for the human reviewer and the Critic. Do NOT
paste file contents in your reply.

Research ambition and problem interpretation. Your goal is to resolve
the given question to the fullest extent possible, with the ideal
outcome being a complete and rigorous solution. The solution may
require novel, creative, and non-trivial elements. If a non-trivial
lemma, computation, or reduction appears necessary, attempt to prove
the lemma, run the computation, or carry out the reduction in this turn
before listing it as a gap. Do not stop at "this remains to be proved"
while you still have a plausible route to attack it.

If the problem statement is ambiguous, inconsistent, missing
definitions, or likely contains a typo, begin answer.tex with a short
section titled "Problem statement and interpretation". State the
cleanest faithful correction or restatement, list any assumptions, and
do not silently solve a different problem. Record the same
interpretation in research_notes.tex so later rounds preserve it. If
the problem is already well-defined, no extra interpretation section is
needed.

When open gaps remain in your treatment, end answer.tex with a section
titled "Remaining open issues" (or equivalent). For each gap, state:
(a) what it is mathematically; (b) where in the document it appears;
(c) what was tried this round or earlier; (d) what would be needed to
close it (a specific computation, an alternative approach, a missing
reference, etc.). If rounds remain and any essential mathematical gap
is still open, keep working by focusing on closing that gap or
exploring an alternative approach to the problem.

Readiness rule. Declare ``<ready>true</ready>`` only when you
genuinely believe the answer is a complete rigorous solution to the
stated problem, with no remaining open gaps, no unproved essential
lemmas, and no missing assumptions. Do not declare
``<ready>true</ready>`` merely because the last round has been reached
or because a "Remaining open issues" section honestly lists what is
left; partial final outputs may list open issues, but they are not
ready. Even then the run terminates only if the Critic also concurs.

### Problem ###
Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

### Latest Critic review ###
## Referee report on revised `answer.tex`

### Executive verdict

The revision is **not answer-ready**. It improves honesty and fixes part of the earlier issue by no longer falsely presenting the amalgamated-free-product permanence theorem as a known DKEP result. However, the revised proof explicitly assumes an unproved “Bass–Serre permanence still to be supplied” proposition and ends with a “Remaining open issues” section. Under the stated readiness criterion, this alone is fatal.

### LaTeX contract check

I compiled the revised `answer.tex` with `pdflatex`. It compiles successfully and produces a **3-page** PDF. It uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, uses the permitted `fullpage` package, and I found no forbidden margin, line-spacing, or in-document font-size commands. The LaTeX contract appears satisfied.

### Literature validation

- The DKEP paper indeed defines \(S(M)\) and proper proximality in the von Neumann algebraic sense used here: \(S(M)\) is an operator system and \(M\)-bimodule, and proper proximality excludes an \(M\)-central state on \(S(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Proposition 6.7 proves proper proximality for **free products of diffuse tracial von Neumann algebras**, not the finite-dimensional scalar-free-product lemma needed here. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The revision addresses this by giving a separate proof.
- The CDHJKN relative amenability theorem used in the graph-product step is accurately represented mathematically: their Main Theorem 0.1 gives the tracial relative amenability criterion with the amenability condition and the two-dimensional exceptional-pair condition. ([content.ems.press](https://content.ems.press/assets/public/full-texts/serials/prims/61/4/14299216/online/10.4171-prims-61-4-3.pdf)) However, the revised `answer.tex` cites it as “Theorem A”; in the published PRIMS version it appears as **Main Theorem 0.1**, so the citation label should be corrected.
- The missing Proposition \(\ref{prop:BS}\) is still not validated by the cited DKEP source. A text search of the DKEP PDF for “amalgamated” and “Bass” produced no matching text. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) A related Ding–Kunnawalkam Elayavalli paper does contain a relative-to-full proper-proximality upgrading theorem, but its Theorem 1.1 has substantial additional hypotheses — diffuse ambient algebra and subalgebras, commuting Jones projections, bounded Pimsner–Popa bases, mixing — and gives a central-projection decomposition, not the clean permanence statement asserted in Proposition \(\ref{prop:BS}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

## Previous concerns: addressed vs. remaining

### Addressed

1. **The previously unvalidated free-product permanence theorem is no longer quoted as a black box.**  
   The author now gives Proposition \(\ref{prop:scalar-free}\), a scalar/free-product lemma designed to handle the finite-dimensional base case.

2. **The previous false confidence about DKEP amalgamated-free-product permanence has been removed.**  
   The revised draft explicitly labels the needed Bass–Serre permanence proposition as “still to be supplied.”

3. **The graph-theoretic induction has been streamlined.**  
   The ordering of vertices by connected induced complement subgraphs is correct.

4. **The CDHJKN relative amenability obstruction remains essentially correct.**  
   The use of the exceptional-pair alternative to contradict connectedness of the induced complement graph is valid.

### Still remaining and fatal

The proof of the actual theorem is conditional on Proposition \(\ref{prop:BS}\):

\[
P\text{ properly proximal and not amenable relative to }B
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
P*_B(B\bar\otimes A)\text{ properly proximal}.
\]

This proposition is neither proved nor cited from a source with matching hypotheses. Since it is used at every induction step after the base case, the proof does not establish the theorem.

## Paragraph-by-paragraph audit

### Problem statement and interpretation

The interpretation of irreducibility as “not a non-trivial join,” equivalently connected complement graph, is standard and faithful to the intended graph-product usage. This part is acceptable.

### Scalar free-product input

Proposition \(\ref{prop:scalar-free}\) is a meaningful improvement over the first draft. The proof adapts the DKEP paradoxical-projection argument from the diffuse setting. The main idea appears correct: the projections onto \(A\)-initial and \(B\)-initial reduced words lie in \(S(A*B)\); two orthogonal trace-zero unitaries in \(A\) and one trace-zero unitary in \(B\) produce a paradoxical inequality; and the vacuum projection is killed using the Haar unitary \(a_1b\).

There are some minor issues to fix if this were part of a final proof:

- The sentence “Exactly as in DKEP” is still compressed. It should explicitly verify that the trace-zero parts of \(A\) and \(B\), together with scalars, ultraweakly generate \(A*B\), and that the relevant commutators and adjoint commutators lie in \(K_{\infty,1}\).
- The proof should explicitly note that \(S(N)\) is an \(N\)-bimodule, so \(a_iP_Ba_i^*\), \(bP_Ab^*\), and \(h^ne_{\mathbb C}h^{-n}\) all lie in \(S(N)\).
- The line “Centrality implies \(N\varphi(e_{\mathbb C})\le1\) for every \(N\)” uses \(N\) both as the algebra and as an integer. It should say \(m\varphi(e_{\mathbb C})\le1\) for every \(m\in\mathbb N\).

These are fixable, and I do not regard them as the main obstruction. The scalar base case is now plausibly handled.

### Graph-product reduction

The CDHJKN theorem is used correctly, but the theorem number/citation should be changed from “Theorem A” to the corresponding theorem number in the cited published version, namely Main Theorem 0.1. The graph-product AFP decomposition

\[
M_{W\cup\{v\}}\cong M_W*_{M_L}(M_L\bar\otimes M_v)
\]

is standard and correctly stated with the canonical expectations.

### Proposition \(\ref{prop:BS}\)

This is the fatal gap. The proposition is explicitly titled “Bass–Serre permanence still to be supplied.” The proof of the theorem is then introduced with “Assuming Proposition \(\ref{prop:BS}\).” This means the theorem is not proved.

The “Remaining open issues” section confirms the same point: the author acknowledges that a genuine proof or exact literature theorem for Proposition \(\ref{prop:BS}\) is still needed. Under the user’s readiness criterion, a solution containing such an open issue must be marked not ready.

### Base case

Given Proposition \(\ref{prop:scalar-free}\), the base case is valid. If \(v_2\) is nonadjacent to both \(v_1\) and \(v_3\), then

\[
M_{V_3}\cong M_{\{v_1,v_3\}}*M_{v_2}.
\]

The unitaries \(u_1\in M_{v_1}\) and \(u_3\in M_{v_3}\) are trace-zero and orthogonal in \(M_{\{v_1,v_3\}}\), whether \(v_1\) and \(v_3\) are adjacent or not. Thus the scalar-free-product proposition applies.

### Inductive step

The graph-theoretic part of the induction is correct. Since the induced complement graph on \(V_{k-1}\) is connected and has at least three vertices, a complement neighbor \(r\) of \(v_k\) inside \(V_{k-1}\) has a complement neighbor \(s\) inside \(V_{k-1}\). Since \(r\notin L\) and \(r,s\) are nonadjacent in \(\Gamma\), the CDHJKN criterion forces the two-dimensional exceptional alternative if \(P\) were amenable relative to \(M_L\). That would isolate \(\{r,s\}\) as a connected component of the induced complement graph, contradicting connectedness.

Thus the proof successfully proves:

\[
P=M_{V_{k-1}}\text{ is not amenable relative to }M_L.
\]

But the passage from this fact to proper proximality of

\[
M_{V_k}\cong P*_{M_L}(M_L\bar\otimes M_{v_k})
\]

uses the unproved Proposition \(\ref{prop:BS}\). Therefore the induction is conditional only.

### Remaining open issues section

This section is incompatible with answer-ready status. The document explicitly says:

> “The proof above is complete except for Proposition \(\ref{prop:BS}\).”

That is an admission of an essential missing lemma. The problem asks for a complete rigorous proof, not a conditional reduction.

## New issues introduced by the revision

1. **The proof now explicitly contains an open problem/gap.**  
   This is clearer and more honest than the first draft, but it makes the document formally non-solution.

2. **Citation mismatch for CDHJKN.**  
   The published reference in the bibliography labels the relevant result as Main Theorem 0.1, not Theorem A.

3. **The scalar-free-product proof is somewhat terse.**  
   It likely can be repaired, but as written it still relies on “Exactly as in DKEP” for technical membership in \(S(N)\). This is not the fatal issue, but a strict final version should expand it.

## Final verdict

The revised draft is a useful partial reduction, and the base case is much improved. However, the central amalgamated-free-product permanence statement remains unproved and is explicitly listed as an open issue. Therefore `answer.tex` does **not** fully solve the stated problem.

### Council member: gpt-55-pro (models/openai/gpt-55-pro) ###
I would avoid trying to make the Bass–Serre half-tree projections lie in \(S(M)\). The right framework is “proper proximality relative to \(B\)” plus the DKEP relative-to-absolute upgrade.

Concretely, for the graph-specific situation, I think the following lemma is the clean target.

**Relative Bass–Serre lemma.** Let
\[
M=P*_B Q,\qquad Q=B\bar\otimes A,
\]
with \(u\in Q\ominus B\) a unitary and with unitaries \(v_1,v_2\in P\ominus B\) satisfying
\[
E_B(v_i)=0,\qquad E_B(v_1^*v_2)=0.
\]
Then \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(B\).

Proof sketch: let \(p\) and \(q\) be the projections onto reduced words whose first letter is respectively in \(P\ominus B\) and \(Q\ominus B\). Then
\[
1=e_B+p+q.
\]
For algebraic \(x\in M\), the commutators \([p,JxJ]\), \([q,JxJ]\) lie in the relative compact ideal
\[
\mathcal K_B=\overline{\operatorname{span}}\, M e_B M
\]
on \(L^2(M)\). Thus \(p,q\) define genuine projections in the relative Calkin/boundary algebra modulo \(\mathcal K_B\). In that quotient \(e_B=0\), so \(p+q=1\). The left multiplication estimates are exactly the scalar free-product estimates:
\[
v_1 q v_1^*+v_2 q v_2^*\le p,\qquad u p u^*\le q.
\]
The first uses \(B\)-orthogonality of \(v_1,v_2\); the second uses \(E_B(u)=0\). Hence any \(M\)-central state on the relative boundary algebra would satisfy
\[
2\psi(q)\le \psi(p)\le \psi(q),
\]
so \(\psi(p)=\psi(q)=0\), contradicting \(p+q=1\). This is exactly the relative proper proximality assertion.

Then use the DKEP relative-to-absolute criterion: if \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(B\) and has no nonzero direct summand amenable relative to \(B\), then \(M\) is properly proximal. I am not fully certain of the theorem number, but it is in DKEP’s §4 normal-bidual/boundary-piece formalism; this is the result I would cite, rather than an AFP permanence theorem.

Finally, check the non-relative-amenability input descends correctly. If a nonzero central summand of \(M\) were amenable relative to \(B\), restricting the corresponding central state on \(\langle M,e_B\rangle\) to the expected subalgebra \(P\subset M\) gives relative amenability of a nonzero \(P\)-summand relative to \(B\). So in the induction you should phrase the hypothesis as “no nonzero direct summand of \(P\) is amenable relative to \(B\)” or explicitly justify this strengthening from the graph criterion.

For the full abstract proposition without the two \(B\)-orthogonal \(P\)-unitaries, I am less confident. Proper proximality of \(P\) should be usable by inducing DKEP boundary pieces from \(P\) to the \(P\)-vertex of the Bass–Serre decomposition, but that is a substantially heavier argument. For your graph application, the explicit two-unitary relative paradoxical argument is the safer rigorous route.

### Council member: opus_47_max (models/anthropic/opus_47_max) ###
Three angles, in order of how I'd try them.

**(1) First, re-read CDHJKN's main theorems before doing further work.** Their Theorem A (which you cite) is the relative amenability classification, but the structural payoff theorems of "On the structure of graph product von Neumann algebras" almost certainly include proper proximality of irreducible graph products as a stated consequence — they typically list it alongside primeness/solidity. There is a real chance Prop BS (or directly your conclusion) is one of their numbered results with a slightly different label. *Uncertain* on the exact theorem number, but I'd bet odds it's in there.

**(2) If you do prove Prop BS yourself, the right framework is DKEP boundary pieces.** Set $\mathbb{K}_B \subset \mathbb{B}(L^2 M)$ = the compact ideal of $\langle M,e_B\rangle$ (norm closure of $\overline{Me_BM}$). With two $B$-orthogonal, $B$-centered unitaries $u,v \in P\ominus B$ (graph-specific!) and one trace-zero $a\in A$:

  - The half-tree projections $P_1, P_2$ (onto AFP words with first letter from $P\ominus B$ vs.\ $B\bar\otimes A\ominus B$) lie in $\widetilde{\mathbb{S}}_{\mathbb{K}_B}(M)$, as you note.
  - Paradoxical inequalities: $u P_2 u^* + v P_2 v^* \leq P_1$ (use $E_B(v^*u)=0$ to get orthogonality of the $u$- and $v$-images in $\mathrm{Range}(P_1)$ via the $B$-Hilbert-module structure on $L^2(P)\ominus L^2(B)$) and $a P_1 a^* \leq P_2$.
  - Haar argument for the vacuum: $h=ua$ has reduced AFP form of length $2n$ alternating, so $E_B(h^n)=0$ for $n\ne 0$, hence $\{h^n L^2(B)\}_n$ pairwise orthogonal, hence any $M$-central $\varphi$ on $\mathbb{S}_{\mathbb{K}_B}(M)$ has $\varphi(e_B)=0$. Combined with $P_1 + P_2 + e_B = 1$, no such $\varphi$ exists.

So $M$ is $\mathbb{K}_B$-properly proximal.

**(3) Upgrade $\mathbb{K}_B$-pp to pp using non-amenability of $P$ rel $B$.** The clean folklore statement:

> *If $M$ is $\mathbb{K}_B$-properly proximal and $M$ is not amenable relative to $B$ inside $M$, then $M$ is properly proximal.*

Sketch: Given an $M$-central $\varphi$ on $\mathbb{S}(M)$ with normal $M$-restriction, look at $\varphi$ on the image of $\mathbb{K}_B\cap\widetilde{\mathbb{S}}(M)$. Vanishing there lets $\varphi$ descend to an $M$-central state on $\mathbb{S}_{\mathbb{K}_B}(M)$, contradicting (2). Non-vanishing gives (via Hahn–Banach + a Day-style averaging) an $M$-central state on $\langle M, e_B\rangle$ with normal restriction to $M$, i.e., $M$ amenable rel $B$ inside $M$. Then $M$ amenable rel $B$ implies $P$ amenable rel $B$ inside $P$ (restrict to $\langle P, e_B\rangle \subset \langle M, e_B\rangle$), contradicting your hypothesis. A version of this upgrade — restated as "transferring central states between $\mathbb{S}_X$ and $\mathbb{S}$ uses relative non-amenability" — is essentially the technical content of the relative-to-full upgrade in **DKE, *Structure of relatively bi-exact group vN algebras*** (your DKE_rel_biexact). *Uncertain* whether the precise statement is there verbatim, but the proof template is.

**Other places to look:** any Caspers-and-collaborators paper on proper proximality for Hecke/Coxeter/graph-product algebras — they have been running exactly this Bass–Serre + boundary-piece induction. Also worth searching recent DKE preprints — AFP permanence is a question they have been circling.

**My recommendation:** Don't try to prove Prop BS in full generality. State your AFP permanence as a graph-flavored lemma — "$P$ properly proximal, $P$ not amenable rel $B$, $P$ contains two $B$-orthogonal $B$-centered unitaries, $A$ has trace-zero unitary ⇒ $M$ properly proximal" — and prove it by (2)+(3) cleanly in 2–3 pages. This avoids citation gambling on whether the abstract AFP statement is in the literature.

### Council member: gemini-31-pro (models/gemini/gemini-31-pro) ###
Here is a rigorous, self-contained proof strategy that bridges your gap perfectly. It uses exactly the graph-supplied unitaries you identified and the boundary piece quotienting typical of DKEP, sidestepping the need for an exact literature match. 

The crux of the gap is that the Bass--Serre half-tree projections $P_P$ and $P_Q$ (where $Q = B \bar\otimes A$) do not commute with the right action $J_M M J_M$, so they do not naturally live in the proper proximality boundary $S(M)$. However, their commutators with $J_M M J_M$ fall into the relative compact ideal $J_B = \overline{M e_B M} \subset \mathbb{B}(L^2M)$. 

You can upgrade this to full proper proximality using your non-relative-amenability hypothesis in the following sequence:

**1. Set up the Adjoint Action:**
Proper proximality of $M$ is equivalent to the non-existence of a state $\varphi$ on $\mathbb{B}(L^2M)$ that vanishes on the compacts $\mathbb{K}(L^2M)$, has normal restriction to $M$, and is invariant under the adjoint action $\text{Ad}(x)(T) = x J_M x J_M T J_M x^* J_M x^*$ for all unitaries $x \in M$. Assume such a $\varphi$ exists.

**2. Quotient by the Relative Boundary $J_B$:**
Because $P$ is not amenable relative to $B$, $M$ is also not amenable relative to $B$. By standard DKEP theory (cf. DKEP '23, Section 4), any $\text{Ad}(M)$-invariant state on $\mathbb{B}(L^2M)$ that gives non-zero weight to $J_B$ restricts to an $M$-central state on the basic construction $\langle M, e_B \rangle$, which would force $M$ to be amenable relative to $B$. Since this is false, your hypothetical state MUST have $\varphi(J_B) = 0$.

Thus, $\varphi$ drops to a well-defined, $\text{Ad}(M)$-invariant state on the quotient $X_B = \mathbb{B}(L^2M) / J_B$. 

**3. The Paradoxical Argument in $X_B$:**
Let $\pi : \mathbb{B}(L^2M) \to X_B$ be the quotient map. Because the commutators $[P_P, J_M x J_M]$ and $[P_Q, J_M x J_M]$ are in $J_B$, the projections $\pi(P_P)$ and $\pi(P_Q)$ strictly commute with the right action of $M$ inside $X_B$. 

Because they commute with the right action in the quotient, the left-action translations become equivalent to the adjoint-action translations in $X_B$:
\[
\text{For } u \in P, \quad \pi(u P_Q u^*) = \pi(u J_M u J_M P_Q J_M u^* J_M u^*) = \text{Ad}(u)(\pi(P_Q)).
\]

Now, use your graph-specific $B$-orthogonal unitaries $u, v \in P \ominus B$ and $w \in Q \ominus B$. As you noted, the left-action geometry yields $u P_Q u^* \le P_P$ and $v P_Q v^* \le P_P$ with orthogonal ranges, plus $w P_P w^* \le P_Q$. 

Pushing this down to $X_B$, the $\text{Ad}(M)$-invariance of $\varphi$ immediately yields:
\[
2\varphi(\pi(P_Q)) \le \varphi(\pi(P_P)) \quad \text{and} \quad \varphi(\pi(P_P)) \le \varphi(\pi(P_Q)).
\]
This forces $\varphi(\pi(P_P)) = \varphi(\pi(P_Q)) = 0$.

**4. The Contradiction:**
Since $P_P + P_Q + e_B = 1$ in $\mathbb{B}(L^2M)$, applying $\pi$ gives $\pi(P_P) + \pi(P_Q) + \pi(e_B) = 1_{X_B}$. But $e_B \in J_B$, so $\pi(e_B) = 0$. 
This implies $0 + 0 + 0 = \varphi(1) = 1$, a contradiction.

This cleanly bridges the gap: the relative compact ideal $J_B$ traps the failure of the projections to live in $S(M)$, and the hypothesis that $P$ is not amenable relative to $B$ is exactly what allows you to bypass $J_B$ entirely.

## Verdict

I did **not** find an exact published/preprint theorem proving the needed permanence statement
\[
  P\text{ properly proximal and not amenable relative to }B
  \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
  P*_B(B\bar\otimes A)\text{ properly proximal}
\]
for arbitrary separable tracial \(B\subset P\) with expectation and arbitrary tracial \(A\) containing a trace-zero unitary.

The closest available results are genuine near misses: they upgrade relative proper proximality to full proper proximality only under additional **mixing**, **bounded Pimsner--Popa basis**, **biexactness/weak exactness**, or **injective amalgam** assumptions. These hypotheses do not hold in the graph-product induction in general, especially because \(B\subset B\bar\otimes A\) is not mixing when \(B\) is diffuse and \(A\ne \mathbb C\).

## Literature checked

### 1. DKEP, *Properly proximal von Neumann algebras*, Duke Math. J. 172 (2023), 2821--2894; arXiv:2204.00517v3

Relevant items:

- Theorem 6.2: equivalent formulations of proper proximality relative to a boundary piece \(X\), via central states on \(S_X(M)\), central projections, and u.c.p. maps into basic constructions.
- Proposition 6.7: if \(M_1,M_2\) are **diffuse** tracial von Neumann algebras, then \(M_1*M_2\) is properly proximal.
- Theorem 6.11: if \(Q\subset M\) is regular and \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_Q\), then weakly compact regular \(P\subset M\) must intertwine into \(Q\).
- Proposition 9.1 and Section 9: malleable-deformation criteria give proper proximality **relative to a boundary piece** when the deformation is compact relative to that boundary.

This paper does not contain the desired AFP permanence theorem. Proposition 6.7 is non-amalgamated and requires both factors diffuse, so it does not cover finite-dimensional \(A\), e.g. \(A=\mathbb C^2\), nor arbitrary \(B\).

### 2. Ding--Kunnawalkam Elayavalli, *Structure of relatively bi-exact group von Neumann algebras*, Comm. Math. Phys. 405 (2024), Paper 104; arXiv:2211.05298v3

Main near miss: Theorem 1.1.

Hypotheses, in the form relevant here:

- \(M\) diffuse finite.
- \(M_i\subset M\), \(i=1,\dots,n\), are diffuse subalgebras.
- The Jones projections \(e_{M_i}\) pairwise commute.
- Each \(M_i\subset M\) admits a bounded Pimsner--Popa basis.
- \(A\subset pMp\) is properly proximal relative to the boundary piece \(X\) generated by the \(M_i\)'s.
- Each \(M_i\subset M\) is mixing.

Conclusion: central projections split \(A\) into a properly proximal summand and summands amenable relative to the \(M_i\)'s.

Why it does not apply: the target boundary is the Bass--Serre boundary \(X_B\), and the relevant subalgebra \(B\) is not generally diffuse/mixing with bounded Pimsner--Popa basis. If one tries \(M_i=P\) and \(M_i=B\bar\otimes A\), the mixing assumptions also fail in general for AFPs over diffuse \(B\). This theorem is an upgrade theorem, but not under the hypotheses of Proposition BS.

### 3. Ding--Peterson, *Biexact von Neumann algebras*, arXiv:2309.10161

Relevant items:

- Definition 6.1: biexactness relative to a boundary piece.
- Corollary 6.11: if \(M\) is biexact, every subalgebra is either properly proximal or has a nonzero amenable summand.
- Proposition 6.13: if \(M\) is biexact relative to a family \(\{B_j\}\), then for finite \(N\subset M\), either \(N'\cap M\) is amenable or \(N\prec_M B_j\) for some \(j\).
- Proposition 6.16: non-amalgamated free products of weakly exact von Neumann algebras are biexact relative to the two factors; if the factors are biexact, the free product is biexact.

This does not prove the needed permanence: it gives no arbitrary-amalgam AFP theorem, and relative biexactness alone gives solidity-type conclusions, not full proper proximality without an additional upgrade theorem.

### 4. Toyosawa--Yang, *On Relative Biexactness of Amalgamated Free Product von Neumann Algebras*, arXiv:2505.19508v2

This is the most relevant later preprint I found.

Relevant results:

- Theorem 1.1: for AFPs \(M_1*_B M_2\) with **injective** common \(B\), the embedding into \(\langle M_1,e_B\rangle *_B \langle M_2,e_B\rangle\) is relatively nuclear.
- Corollary 1.2 / Proposition 4.15: if \(M_i\) are weakly exact tracial and \(B\) is injective, then \(M_1*_B M_2\) is biexact relative to \(\{M_1,M_2\}\). Proposition 4.15 includes finite-dimensional \(B\) as a case.
- Theorem 1.3 / 4.17: if the \(M_i\) are injective, then \(M_1*_B M_2\) is biexact relative to \(B\).
- Theorem 1.4 / 5.16: if \(M\) is biexact relative to \(X_F\) for a finite family \(F=\{N_i\}\) of **mixing** subalgebras, then any \(A\subset pMp\) splits into properly proximal, amenable, and relatively amenable pieces.
- Lemma 5.5 and Corollary 5.7: in an AFP, \(M_1\subset M_1*_B M_2\) is mixing if \(B\subset M_2\) is mixing; all factors and \(B\) are mixing if \(B\) is mixing in every \(M_i\).

Why it does not apply: in our target \(M_2=B\bar\otimes A\), the inclusion \(B\subset B\bar\otimes A\) is not mixing when \(B\) is diffuse and \(A\ne \mathbb C\). Indeed, for \(0\ne a\in A\ominus\mathbb C\) and weakly-null unitaries \(u_n\in B\),
\[
  E_B((1\otimes a)u_n(1\otimes a^*))=\tau_A(aa^*)u_n,
\]
which does not converge to \(0\) in \(L^2(B)\). Thus the Toyosawa--Yang upgrade theorem does not cover arbitrary \(B\), and its relative-biexactness results require injective \(B\) and weak exactness/injectivity assumptions absent from the graph-product induction.

### 5. Ding--Kunnawalkam Elayavalli, *Proper proximality among various families of groups*, Groups Geom. Dyn. 18 (2024), 921--938; arXiv:2107.02917

The group Bass--Serre results are also not an exact von Neumann algebra theorem. Theorem 1.2 proves proper proximality for groups acting non-elementarily on trees when intersections of pairs of edge stabilizers are finite. Corollaries include amalgamated free products/HNN extensions under weak malnormality/index hypotheses. These are stronger than mere non-coamenability of \(H<G\), the group analogue of \(L(G)\) not amenable relative to \(L(H)\).

## What can be proved cleanly: relative Bass--Serre proper proximality

The graph-specific extra unitaries do prove the expected **relative** statement.

**Lemma.** Let \(M=P*_B Q\), \(Q=B\bar\otimes A\), with trace-preserving expectations. Suppose \(A\) has a trace-zero unitary \(u\), and \(P\) contains two unitaries \(w_1,w_2\in P\ominus B\) such that \(E_B(w_i^*w_j)=0\) for \(i\ne j\). Let \(X_B\) be the DKEP boundary piece generated by \(e_B\). Then \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\).

Proof outline. Decompose \(L^2(M)\) as the AFP Fock space over \(B\). Let \(p_P,p_Q\) be the projections onto reduced words whose first letter is respectively from \(P\ominus B\) and \(Q\ominus B\). The usual Bass--Serre commutator computation gives
\[
  p_P,p_Q\in S_{X_B}(M),
\]
because right multiplication can change the first letter only through cancellations detected by \(e_B\). If \(\varphi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\), then left multiplication gives
\[
  w_1p_Qw_1^*+w_2p_Qw_2^*\le p_P,\qquad
  up_Pu^*\le p_Q.
\]
The first inequality uses the \(B\)-orthogonality of \(w_1,w_2\); the second uses \(u\in Q\ominus B\). Centrality gives
\[
  2\varphi(p_Q)\le \varphi(p_P)\le \varphi(p_Q),
\]
so \(\varphi(p_P)=\varphi(p_Q)=0\). The remaining projection is \(e_B=1-p_P-p_Q\). The unitary \(h=w_1u\) is \(B\)-Haar in the sense that \(h^nL^2(B)\), \(n\in\mathbb Z\), are pairwise orthogonal \(B\)-submodules; hence the projections \(h^n e_B h^{-n}\) are pairwise orthogonal. Centrality gives \(\varphi(e_B)=0\), contradiction.

This is exactly the Bass--Serre paradoxical argument, but only in \(S_{X_B}(M)\), not in \(S(M)\).

## Where the full proof still breaks

For arbitrary \(B\), the half-tree projections \(p_P,p_Q\) need not lie in \(S(M)\). The obstruction is concrete: for \(x\in P\ominus B\), right multiplication by \(x\) on a reduced word ending in \(P\ominus B\) can replace the last letter by a \(B\)-coefficient \(E_B(\cdot x)\), shortening the word and changing the first letter. This gives an operator compact relative to \(e_B\), but not compact in the scalar \(K_{\infty,1}(M)\)-sense when \(B\) is infinite-dimensional.

Thus a central state on \(S(M)\) cannot currently be evaluated on \(p_P,p_Q\). The missing upgrade would have to show that non-relative amenability of \(P\) over \(B\), plus proper proximality of \(P\), converts this relative contradiction into an ordinary one. The existing DKE/DKEP/Toyosawa--Yang upgrade mechanisms require mixing or bounded-basis hypotheses that are unavailable here.

## A valid replacement statement: finite-dimensional amalgam

The above proof does give an ordinary proper proximality result when \(B\) is finite-dimensional.

**Proposition.** If \(B\) is finite-dimensional and the graph-specific hypotheses above hold, then
\[
  P*_B(B\bar\otimes A)
\]
is properly proximal.

Reason: when \(B\) is finite-dimensional, \(e_B\) is finite-rank and \(X_B\subset K(L^2M)\). Hence \(p_P,p_Q\in S(M)\), and the relative Bass--Serre paradoxical argument above is an ordinary DKEP central-state contradiction.

This covers finite-dimensional amalgams and finite-dimensional \(A\), but it does not close the induction in `answer.tex`, where \(B=M_L\) can be infinite-dimensional.

## Counterexample search

I did not find a concrete counterexample to Proposition BS itself. The obvious tensor-product attempt is excluded by the hypothesis that \(P\) is properly proximal: if \(P=N\bar\otimes R\) with \(R\) diffuse amenable and \(B=R\), then
\[
  P*_B(B\bar\otimes A)\cong R\bar\otimes(N*A),
\]
which is not properly proximal when \(R\) is diffuse amenable, but \(P\) is already not properly proximal by DKEP's tensor/product and property-Gamma obstructions.

What is false is several stronger routes one might hope to use:

- automatic mixing of the amalgam \(B\) in \(B\bar\otimes A\) is false for diffuse \(B\);
- automatic relative-biexactness of AFPs over arbitrary \(B\) is not known and Toyosawa--Yang require injective \(B\);
- relative biexactness does not automatically upgrade to actual biexactness/proper proximality without mixing-type hypotheses.

## Recommendation for `answer.tex`

Do not cite Proposition BS as established. A safe insertion is:

1. Add the relative Bass--Serre lemma above.
2. Add the finite-dimensional-\(B\) proposition as a proved special case.
3. Leave the arbitrary-\(B\) induction step explicitly conditional on the following still-open upgrade:
   a central state on \(S(M)\) for \(M=P*_B(B\bar\otimes A)\) should induce either a \(P\)-central state on \(\langle P,e_B\rangle\) or a \(P\)-central state on \(S(P)\). I was not able to prove this induction-critical upgrade from the current literature.

        E_{M_{L_v}}(w_1^*w_2)=E_{M_{L_v}}(u_r^*u_su_r)=0,
\]
because the displayed words are reduced and contain the outside vertex $r$.
Thus $M$ is properly proximal relative to $X_{L_v}$ for every $v$.

It remains to note that $M$ is a non-amenable factor.  By
\cite[Main Theorem 0.5]{CDHJKN}, factoriality can fail only if there is a
vertex adjacent to all other vertices, or a non-adjacent pair adjacent to all
remaining vertices.  Either situation would give an isolated vertex, respectively
an isolated edge, in the connected complement graph $\Gamma^c$, impossible since
$|V|\ge3$.  Thus $M$ is a factor.  By \cite[Main Theorem 0.1]{CDHJKN}, if $M$
were amenable, then every non-edge $r\not\sim s$ would have to be one of the
two-dimensional exceptional pairs adjacent to all other vertices.  Such a pair
would again be an isolated component of $\Gamma^c$, contradicting connectedness
and $|V|\ge3$.  Hence $M$ is non-amenable.

The boundary pieces $X_{L_v}$ fill the boundary by Lemma \ref{lem:links-fill}.
Since $M$ is properly proximal relative to each of them, Lemma
\ref{lem:finite-boundary} gives that $M$ is properly proximal.

\begin{thebibliography}{9}

\bibitem{DKEP}
C.~Ding, S.~Kunnawalkam Elayavalli, and J.~Peterson,
\emph{Properly proximal von Neumann algebras},
Duke Math. J. \textbf{172} (2023), 2821--2894.

\bibitem{CDHJKN}
I.~Charlesworth, R.~de Santiago, B.~Hayes, D.~Jekel,
S.~Kunnawalkam Elayavalli, and B.~Nelson,
\emph{On the structure of graph product von Neumann algebras},
Publ. Res. Inst. Math. Sci. \textbf{61} (2025), 713--762.

\end{thebibliography}

\end{document}
"""
Path('/mnt/data/answer.tex').write_text(answer)
